text:
COMPREHENSIVE GENOMIC PROFILING REPORT
Germline Genetic Testing & Somatic Next-Generation Sequencing

в”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓ
PATIENT & SPECIMEN INFORMATION
в”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓ
Patient Name: Raymond Kowalski
Date of Birth: [REDACTED] | Age: 68 years
Sex: Male
Ordering Physician: [Attending Oncologist]
Indication: Newly diagnosed metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC), Gleason 9 (Grade Group 5), PSA 187 ng/mL; family history of BRCA2-associated malignancies (brother: pancreatic cancer; sister: breast cancer); comprehensive genomic profiling requested to guide systemic therapy planning and assess hereditary cancer risk.

SPECIMEN A вЂ” GERMLINE TESTING
Specimen Type: Peripheral blood (EDTA)
Test Performed: Germline multi-gene hereditary cancer panel (BRCA1/2, ATM, CHEK2, PALB2, NBN, BRIP1, RAD51C, RAD51D, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, CDH1, STK11, TP53, PTEN вЂ” 34-gene panel)
Laboratory: [CLIA-certified molecular genetics laboratory]
Turnaround: 14 days

SPECIMEN B вЂ” SOMATIC TUMOR PROFILING
Specimen Type: Formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) prostate needle biopsy tissue (transrectal ultrasound-guided, 12-core; blocks submitted from right apex and right mid-gland cores with highest tumor burden)
Tumor Cellularity (estimated): 60%
Test Performed: Somatic comprehensive genomic profiling, 84-gene NGS panel (DNA-seq; hybrid-capture; includes SNVs, indels, copy number alterations, select gene fusions, and TMB/MSI assessment)
Laboratory: [CLIA/CAP-accredited molecular pathology laboratory]
Turnaround: 18 days

в”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓ
SECTION I: GERMLINE FINDINGS (Specimen A)
в”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓ

PATHOGENIC VARIANT IDENTIFIED

Gene: BRCA2
Variant: c.5946delT (NM_000059.4)
Protein Change: p.Ser1982Argfs*22
Variant Classification: PATHOGENIC
Inheritance: Autosomal dominant
Zygosity: Heterozygous (germline)
Variant Allele Frequency (germline): ~50% (consistent with constitutional heterozygosity)
dbSNP / ClinVar: ClinVar accession VCV000052756; classified as Pathogenic by multiple submitters
Functional Impact: Single nucleotide deletion in exon 11 resulting in a frameshift and premature stop codon at codon 2004 (22 codons downstream of the frameshift). Predicted to cause nonsense-mediated mRNA decay and/or produce a severely truncated non-functional protein lacking critical RAD51-binding domains essential for homologous recombination DNA repair.
Population Frequency: Rare; founder variant not identified in gnomAD population databases at appreciable frequency.
Interpretation: This pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift variant is well-established as a cause of Hereditary Breast and Ovarian Cancer (HBOC) syndrome. In males, germline BRCA2 pathogenic variants are associated with significantly elevated lifetime risk for prostate cancer (up to 8.6-fold increased risk), pancreatic cancer, and male breast cancer. This variant is consistent with the reported family history (pancreatic cancer in brother, breast cancer in sister) and is likely the underlying hereditary etiology for this patient's prostate cancer. NCCN guidelines recommend cascade genetic testing for first- and second-degree relatives.

ALL OTHER GERMLINE GENES TESTED: No pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants identified.
Variants of Uncertain Significance (VUS): ATM c.3161C>T (p.A1054V) вЂ” variant of uncertain significance; insufficient evidence to reclassify; clinical correlation advised. All other genes: negative.

в”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓ
SECTION II: SOMATIC TUMOR GENOMIC PROFILING (Specimen B)
в”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓ

QUALITY METRICS
Mean Coverage Depth: 612x
Percent Bases >100x: 98.7%
Tumor Fraction (bioinformatic estimate): 58%
Sequencing Quality: PASS вЂ” all QC thresholds met

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
SOMAT IC ALTERATIONS DETECTED вЂ” CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ

1. BRCA2 вЂ” p.Ser1982Argfs*22 (c.5946delT)
   Variant Type: Frameshift deletion
   VAF: 78%
   Classification: Pathogenic (somatic; same sequence as germline)
   Interpretation: The markedly elevated somatic VAF (78%) compared to the germline heterozygous state (~50%) is consistent with somatic loss of heterozygosity (LOH) at the BRCA2 locus вЂ” i.e., the wild-type allele has been lost in the tumor through copy-neutral LOH or segmental deletion, resulting in biallelic BRCA2 inactivation. This confirms complete functional loss of BRCA2 in the tumor (homologous recombination deficiency, HRD). Biallelic BRCA2 loss is a predictive biomarker for response to PARP inhibitors (olaparib, rucaparib) in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) per FDA-approved indications (PROfound trial data); it also confers sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy.

2. TP53 вЂ” p.R248W (c.742C>T)
   Variant Type: Missense (hotspot)
   VAF: 42%
   Classification: Pathogenic (somatic)
   Interpretation: Gain-of-function hotspot mutation in the DNA-binding domain of TP53, one of the most frequently encountered TP53 mutations in human cancers. p.R248W abrogates sequence-specific DNA binding and is associated with dominant-negative suppression of remaining wild-type p53 alleles and potential gain-of-function oncogenic activity. TP53 mutations in prostate cancer are enriched in high-grade (Grade Group 4-5) and metastatic disease and correlate with more aggressive clinical behavior, treatment resistance, and poorer prognosis. The VAF (42%) is consistent with heterozygous somatic mutation without apparent LOH.

3. PTEN вЂ” Homozygous Deletion
   Variant Type: Copy number loss (homozygous deletion)
   VAF/Copy Number: 0 copies (deletion confirmed across multiple exons; no residual signal)
   Classification: Pathogenic (somatic)
   Interpretation: Complete biallelic loss of PTEN, a critical negative regulator of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling axis. PTEN deletion occurs in approximately 40-50% of metastatic prostate cancers and is associated with Grade Group 4-5 disease, resistance to androgen receptor-targeted therapy, and activation of the PI3K pathway as an escape mechanism. Co-occurrence with BRCA2 biallelic loss and TP53 mutation defines a high-genomic-risk phenotype. PTEN loss may have implications for sensitivity to PI3K/AKT inhibitors (investigational in prostate cancer; e.g., ipatasertib in combination with abiraterone, IMPAKT trial context).

4. RB1 вЂ” Heterozygous Deletion
   Variant Type: Copy number loss (heterozygous, single-copy deletion)
   VAF/Copy Number: 1 copy (monoallelic)
   Classification: Likely Pathogenic (somatic)
   Interpretation: Monoallelic RB1 deletion detected. Complete biallelic RB1 loss is strongly associated with treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-NEPC) transdifferentiation, particularly when co-occurring with TP53 mutations. While currently only one allele is deleted (heterozygous), this finding вЂ” in the context of concurrent TP53 p.R248W вЂ” raises concern for evolving genomic instability and potential future neuroendocrine transdifferentiation under therapeutic selection pressure. Monitoring for neuroendocrine markers (chromogranin A, NSE) and clinical features is warranted.

5. MYC вЂ” Copy Number Gain
   Variant Type: Copy number amplification
   Copy Number: 6 (diploid reference: 2)
   Classification: Likely Pathogenic (somatic)
   Interpretation: MYC amplification/gain at chromosome 8q24, observed in approximately 20-30% of metastatic prostate cancers. MYC overexpression drives cell cycle progression, ribosome biogenesis, and metabolic reprogramming. In the setting of androgen receptor signaling, MYC cooperates with AR transcriptional programs. MYC gain is associated with resistance to hormone therapies and a more proliferative tumor phenotype.

6. CDK4 вЂ” Copy Number Gain
   Variant Type: Copy number gain
   Copy Number: 4
   Classification: Likely Pathogenic (somatic)
   Interpretation: CDK4 gain on chromosome 12q14. CDK4 is a key regulator of the G1/S cell cycle checkpoint, phosphorylating RB1 to release E2F transcription factors. CDK4 amplification, in the setting of concurrent RB1 heterozygous deletion, may further compromise cell cycle regulation. CDK4/6 inhibitors are not currently approved in prostate cancer but represent a rational investigational target in this molecular context.

7. FOXA1 вЂ” p.R265Q (c.794G>A)
   Variant Type: Missense
   VAF: 31%
   Classification: Likely Pathogenic (somatic)
   Interpretation: Recurrent activating mutation in FOXA1 (Forkhead Box A1), a pioneer transcription factor that regulates androgen receptor chromatin binding and transcriptional activity. FOXA1 mutations, including p.R265Q, are among the most frequently mutated genes in primary and metastatic prostate cancer (occurring in ~10-15% of metastatic cases). This mutation in the Wing2 domain has been shown to alter DNA binding specificity and promote AR-independent transcriptional programs, potentially contributing to endocrine resistance.

8. SPOP вЂ” p.F133V (c.397T>G)
   Variant Type: Missense (hotspot)
   VAF: 18%
   Classification: Likely Pathogenic (somatic)
   Interpretation: SPOP encodes a substrate-recognition subunit of a CUL3-based E3 ubiquitin ligase. Hotspot mutations in the MATH domain (including p.F133V at codon 133) impair interaction with substrates including SRC-3, BRD4, DEK, TRIM24, and AR co-regulators, resulting in their aberrant stabilization. SPOP mutations define a molecular subtype of prostate cancer (the SPOP-mutant subtype, ~10-15% of tumors) with distinct biology. Interestingly, SPOP mutations are mutually exclusive with ETS gene fusions (e.g., TMPRSS2-ERG) in most cases, consistent with the absence of TMPRSS2-ERG fusion in this tumor. The relatively low VAF (18%) may reflect a subclonal event.

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
NEGATIVE FINDINGS вЂ” CLINICALLY RELEVANT GENES TESTED
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
ATM: No pathogenic somatic variant detected; no copy number alteration
CDK12: No pathogenic somatic variant; no deletion
CHEK2: No pathogenic somatic variant
AR (androgen receptor): Wild-type; no amplification; no ligand-binding domain mutations detected
BRCA1: No somatic alteration
PALB2: No somatic alteration
TMPRSS2-ERG fusion: NOT DETECTED
Other ETS fusions (ETV1, ETV4, ETV5, FLI1): NOT DETECTED
IDH1/IDH2: No somatic alteration
MET: No amplification or activating mutation
ERBB2 (HER2): No amplification
BRAF: No somatic mutation
KRAS: No somatic mutation
NRAS: No somatic mutation
APC: No somatic mutation
CTNNB1: No somatic mutation
AXL: No somatic alteration
WWTR1-RSPO2: Not detected

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
IMMUNOTHERAPY BIOMARKERS
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB): 3 mutations/Mb
   Classification: TMB-LOW (threshold for TMB-High: в‰Ґ10 mut/Mb per FDA/NCCN criteria)
   Interpretation: Low tumor mutational burden. TMB-high (в‰Ґ10 mut/Mb) is associated with potential benefit from pembrolizumab per tumor-agnostic FDA approval. This tumor does not meet the TMB-high threshold.

Microsatellite Instability (MSI) Status: MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS)
   Method: NGS-based assessment of 114 microsatellite loci
   Interpretation: No microsatellite instability detected. MSI-high/dMMR tumors are sensitive to immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab); this tumor is MSS, and immunotherapy is not indicated based on this biomarker alone.

PD-L1 Expression: CPS 2 (assessed by IHC on companion tumor block; 22C3 pharmDx assay equivalent)
   Interpretation: CPS <10, not meeting threshold for pembrolizumab benefit in most solid tumor indications. Combined with low TMB and MSS status, immunotherapy is not a recommended therapeutic strategy based on current biomarker data.

Homologous Recombination Deficiency (HRD): POSITIVE
   HRD Score: Elevated (genomic scar analysis positive; LOH score elevated)
   Interpretation: Biallelic BRCA2 loss, in conjunction with elevated genomic scar metrics (LOH, telomeric allelic imbalance, large-scale state transitions), confirms an HRD-positive tumor. This is the most clinically actionable finding in this report.

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
MOLECULAR SUMMARY & INTEGRATED INTERPRETATION
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
This tumor demonstrates a high-genomic-risk molecular profile consistent with Gleason Grade Group 5, metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer. Key features:

  вЂў Biallelic BRCA2 inactivation (germline frameshift + somatic LOH): The dominant actionable finding. This confers HRD-positive status and is associated with sensitivity to PARP inhibitors (olaparib, rucaparib вЂ” FDA-approved in mCRPC setting) and platinum-based chemotherapy. In the current mHSPC setting, PARP inhibitor use is under active investigation (e.g., PROpel, TALAPRO-2, MAGNITUDE trials for abiraterone + PARP inhibitor combinations).

  вЂў TP53 p.R248W (gain-of-function hotspot) + PTEN homozygous deletion: Dual loss of critical tumor suppressors. This combination is associated with aggressive disease biology, shortened response to ADT-based therapies, and possible PI3K pathway activation (relevant given PTEN loss).

  вЂў RB1 heterozygous deletion + TP53 mutation: Early harbingers of potential treatment-emergent neuroendocrine transdifferentiation (t-NEPC). Complete biallelic RB1 loss combined with TP53 mutation is the canonical genomic signature of t-NEPC. Clinical and biomarker surveillance (chromogranin A, NSE, LDH) is recommended during treatment.

  вЂў MYC gain, CDK4 gain, FOXA1 p.R265Q, SPOP p.F133V: Collectively define a proliferative, endocrine-resistant tumor phenotype with potential co-drivers of therapeutic escape.

  вЂў TMPRSS2-ERG fusion absent; ETS-negative tumor: Combined with SPOP mutation (typical in ETS-negative subtype), this is consistent with a SPOP-mutant molecular subtype.

  вЂў MSS, TMB-low, PD-L1 CPS 2: Immunotherapy not supported by current biomarkers.

NOTE ON GERMLINE FINDING: The identification of a germline pathogenic BRCA2 variant has important clinical implications beyond oncologic management, including risk assessment for family members. Genetic counseling referral is strongly recommended. Cascade testing of first-degree relatives (children, siblings) should be offered.

в”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓ
GENES ANALYZED вЂ” SOMATIC PANEL (84-gene panel, partial list)
в”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓ
ABL1, AKT1, AKT2, AKT3, ALK, AR, ATM, ATR, AURKA, AURKB, AXL, BRAF, BRCA1, BRCA2, CDH1, CDK4, CDK6, CDK12, CHEK1, CHEK2, CTNNB1, EGFR, ERBB2, ERBB3, EZH2, FANCA, FANCD2, FANCI, FANCL, FOXA1, GNA11, GNAQ, HRAS, IDH1, IDH2, JAK1, JAK2, JAK3, KIT, KRAS, MAP2K1, MAP2K2, MDM2, MDM4, MET, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, MYC, MYCN, NF1, NF2, NFE2L2, NRAS, PALB2, PDGFRA, PIK3CA, PIK3R1, PMS2, PTEN, RAD51B, RAD51C, RAD51D, RB1, RET, ROS1, RUNX1, SMAD4, SMARCA4, SPOP, STK11, TERT (promoter), TET2, TP53, TSC1, TSC2, VHL, WT1; Select fusions assessed: TMPRSS2-ERG, ETV1/4/5, FGFR fusions, ALK, ROS1, NTRK1/2/3

в”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓ
QUALITY ASSURANCE & LABORATORY CERTIFICATION
в”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓв”Ѓ
This test was developed and its performance characteristics determined by this laboratory. It has not been cleared or approved by the FDA. The FDA has determined that such clearance or approval is not necessary. Variant nomenclature according to HGVS guidelines. Variant classification per ACMG/AMP (germline) and AMP/ASCO/CAP (somatic) criteria. All alterations reported have been reviewed by a board-certified molecular pathologist and/or clinical molecular geneticist.
